note: License-tagged seed interaction facts for the offline ingestion pipeline. Each fact is source-linked and physicianVerified:false (pending sign-off), and expresses the SAME well-established, source-cited class effect already carried by INT-003/004/006 (hepatic enzyme-inducing drugs can reduce contraceptive effectiveness). No new clinical claim is invented; coverage is extended across the contraceptive x enzyme-inducer matrix using closed-class membership. Prose is a non-directive paraphrase of the cited public-domain source. Concept ids reference the existing approved synonym_index ontology; no ontology change.

licenseTags.public_domain: US Government / regulator public-domain guidance (CDC US-MEC, FDA labels, MHRA DSU). Commercial-safe.
licenseTags.curated_prototype: Hand-curated, FSRH-informed prototype record (FSRH tables are copyrighted; content re-expressed, not redistributed).
licenseTags.research_nc: Reserved for CC BY-NC sources (DDInter2, TWOSIDES) — never emitted into a commercial build.

facts.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
facts.potentialConsequence: These agents are hepatic enzyme-inducing drugs. Enzyme induction increases clearance of contraceptive hormones and could reduce the contraceptive effectiveness of combined hormonal contraception. The US Medical Eligibility Criteria classify combined hormonal contraceptives as category 3 during use of these anticonvulsant and other enzyme-inducing therapies.
facts.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
facts.population: People using or considering combined hormonal contraception who take a hepatic enzyme-inducing drug
facts.sourceTitle: U.S. Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use, 2024 — Summary of Classifications
facts.sourceOrganization: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
facts.sourceSection: Summary of Classifications — Anticonvulsant therapy (phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone) and Antiretroviral therapy (efavirenz); CHC = category 3
facts.limitations: Interacting agents are treated as representative hepatic enzyme inducers via closed-class membership. The magnitude of any reduction in contraceptive effectiveness for an individual is not established. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
facts.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership is exactly the single agents enumerated here. Extends CHC coverage beyond the carbamazepine/rifampicin/rifabutin records (INT-001/002). Class words never match by themselves.

facts.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
facts.potentialConsequence: These agents are hepatic enzyme-inducing drugs. Enzyme induction could reduce the contraceptive effectiveness of progestogen-only pills. The US Medical Eligibility Criteria classify progestogen-only pills as category 3 during use of these enzyme-inducing therapies.
facts.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
facts.population: People using or considering a progestogen-only pill who take a hepatic enzyme-inducing drug
facts.sourceTitle: U.S. Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use, 2024 — Summary of Classifications
facts.sourceOrganization: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
facts.sourceSection: Summary of Classifications — Antiretroviral therapy (efavirenz) and other enzyme-inducing drugs; POP row
facts.limitations: Extends INT-003 (which already enumerates carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone, rifampicin, rifabutin) with efavirenz, St John's wort, griseofulvin. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
facts.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership adds the enzyme inducers not enumerated by INT-003.

facts.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
facts.potentialConsequence: These agents are hepatic enzyme-inducing drugs. Enzyme induction could reduce the contraceptive effectiveness of the etonogestrel implant. The US Medical Eligibility Criteria classify the etonogestrel implant as category 2 during use of enzyme-inducing therapies, with an alternative effective method recommended when reduced effectiveness is a concern.
facts.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
facts.population: People using or considering the etonogestrel implant who take a hepatic enzyme-inducing drug
facts.sourceTitle: U.S. Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use, 2024 — Summary of Classifications
facts.sourceOrganization: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
facts.sourceSection: Summary of Classifications — Anticonvulsant / Antiretroviral therapy; Implant row
facts.limitations: Extends INT-004 with efavirenz, St John's wort, griseofulvin. US-MEC implant categorization differs from CHC/POP and is recorded here as severityOrdinal 2. Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
facts.machineMatchingNotes: Closed-class membership adds the enzyme inducers not enumerated by INT-004.

facts.interactionDirection: Interacting medication may reduce exposure to the contraceptive hormone
facts.potentialConsequence: Phenobarbital is a hepatic enzyme inducer. Enzyme induction increases the metabolism of levonorgestrel and reduces its plasma levels, which may reduce the contraceptive efficacy of levonorgestrel-containing emergency contraception. Elevated enzyme levels can persist for up to 4 weeks after the enzyme-inducing medicine is stopped.
facts.clinicianConsideration: Evidence to review in the individual clinical context.
facts.population: People seeking levonorgestrel emergency contraception who have used phenobarbital within the last 4 weeks
facts.sourceTitle: Drug Safety Update: Levonorgestrel-containing emergency hormonal contraception (September 2016)
facts.sourceOrganization: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)
facts.sourceSection: 'Examples of enzyme inducers that reduce plasma levonorgestrel levels' — barbiturates class member phenobarbital
facts.limitations: Enumerates phenobarbital, the barbiturate deliberately left unexpanded in INT-006 (whose list names the class word 'barbiturates' but not the member). Prototype dataset entry; requires verification against the cited source and individual clinical context.
facts.machineMatchingNotes: Enumerates the specific barbiturate member (phenobarbital) that INT-006 left as an unexpanded class word.
